partly destroyed |}
===Nishiyama Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishiyama Kofun'''|西山古墳|}} is located on the grounds of Tenri High School ({{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E|display=inline}}). It is believed to have been built in the early Kofun period (4th century), making it the oldest of the Somanouchi Kofun Group. The tumulus has a unique shape, with the lowest tier having a two-conjoined rectangle {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shape, and the central and upper tiers having a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}} shaped, which look like a keyhole with one square end and one circular end when viewed from above. This is the only known example of such a design in Japan. The lowest tier consists of two square mounds measuring 90 meters on each side. The middle tier has a circular posterior section 72 meters in diameter, and a rectangular anterior portion with a length of 90 meters. The top of the tumulus is flat, with a diameter of 30 meters and continues with a long and narrow front section for seven meters. The rear of the tumulus is 15 meters high and the front section is ten meters high. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is believed to have been a vertical entry pit-type stone chamber, which has been robbed, most recently in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], when the [[:en:Imperial Japanese Army|Imperial Japanese Army]] set up an anti-aircraft gun emplacement and removed many of the stones. The tumulus is orientated to the west, and was once surrounded by a stepped square moat, which has now been filled-in. The tumulus is located on a gentle slope, making effective use of the natural terrain, so the width of the moat is not constant.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Fragments of a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], jasper arrowheads, tubular beads, and fragments of an iron sword and an iron knife have been excavated from the posterior circular portion. A [[:en:Jasper|Jasper]] wheel stone was discovered in the moat on the south side of the anterior portion. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and fragments of ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found, including cylindrical ''haniwa'', finned cylindrical ''haniwa'', and house-shaped ''haniwa''.
<gallery> Nishiyama Kofun, zenkei-1.jpg|Nishiyama Kofun 080515 Nishiyama Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view of NIshiyama Kofun </gallery>
The tumulus is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tenri Station|Tenri Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sakurai Line|Sakurai Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Nishinorikura Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishinorikura Kofun'''|西乗鞍古墳|}} is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}} keyhole-shaped tumulus built by cutting into the foot of a hill stretching from east-to-west on the eastern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]] ({{coord|34|35|9.68|N|135|50|44.56|E|display=inline}}). It is the second largest in the Somanouchi Kofun Group after the Nishiyama Kofun. The tumulus is orientated to the south and is constructed in two tiers. The posterior circular portion has a diameter of 66 meters and height of 15.5 meters; the anterior rectangular port has a width of 88 meters and height of 16 meters. The tumulus was used as an observation point by [[:en:Emperor Hirohito|Emperor Hirohito]] in 1932, when he observed military training maneuvers in the vicinity, and has been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] four times since 1981. Cylindrical and figurative ''haniwa'' clay figures have been found on the outside of the tumulus. There are also projections on the east and west sides of the narrow part of the tumulus. The mound is surrounded by an inner moat and an outer bank (now buried and forming a flat area), and the total length including these is about 165 meters from north-to-south. Furthermore, portions of outer moats are also found on the south and east sides, so it is believed that the tumulus originally had a double moat. The burial chamber is unclear, but it is possible that it was a horizontal-entry stone chamber. The grave goods are also unclear, but [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] pottery have been found with the excavated ''haniwa''. From this pottery, the construction date of the tumulus is estimated to be around the end of the 5th century, or the end of the middle Kofun period.
<gallery> Nishinorikura Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|View of the rear mound from the front part Nishinorikura Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View of the front part from the rear mound 西乗鞍古墳出土 円筒埴輪.JPG|Cylindrical Haniwa excavated from Nishinorikura Kofun </gallery>

{{Coord|34.59275|N|135.84366666666668|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location =[[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Tenri|qid=Q502196}}| region = [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th- 7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|qid=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Somanouchi Kofun cluster'''|杣之内古墳群|}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Somanouchi-cho, Magata-cho, and Otogi-cho neighborhoods of the city of [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The Nishiyama Kofun was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927, and the Nishinokura Kofun was added to the designation in 2018, with the name of the National Historic Site changing from the "Nishiyama Kofun" to the "Somanouchi Kofun cluster".<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210780|title=杣之内古墳群 / 西山古墳 / 西乗鞍古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== Located south of the Furu River, the tumuli are built on hills to the south of [[Isonokami Jingu|Isonokami Shrine]]. The shape of the mounds is characterized by a transition from [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped]] to giant circular tombs. From the location, there is a theory that these the tombs of the chiefs of the [[Mononobe clan]].

===Nishiyama Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishiyama Kofun'''|西山古墳|}} is located on the grounds of Tenri High School ({{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E|display=inline}}). The tumulus has a unique shape, with the lowest tier having a two-conjoined rectangle {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shape, and the central and upper tiers having a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}} shaped, which look like a keyhole with one square end and one circular end when viewed from above. The middle tier has a circular posterior section 72 meters in diameter, and a rectangular anterior portion with a length of 90 meters. The top of the tumulus is flat, with a diameter of 30 meters and continues with a long and narrow front section for seven meters. The rear of the tumulus is 15 meters high and the front section is ten meters high. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is believed to have been a vertical entry pit-type stone chamber, which has been robbed, most recently in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], when the [[:en:Imperial Japanese Army|Imperial Japanese Army]] set up an anti-aircraft gun emplacement and removed many of the stones. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and fragments of ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found, including cylindrical ''haniwa'', finned cylindrical ''haniwa'', and house-shaped ''haniwa''.
<gallery> Nishiyama Kofun, zenkei-1.jpg|Nishiyama Kofun 080515 Nishiyama Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view of NIshiyama Kofun </gallery>
The tumulus is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tenri Station|Tenri Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sakurai Line|Sakurai Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Nishinorikura Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishinorikura Kofun'''|西乗鞍古墳|}} is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun}} keyhole-shaped tumulus built by cutting into the foot of a hill stretching from east-to-west on the eastern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]] ({{coord|34|35|9.68|N|135|50|44.56|E|display=inline}}). {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|qid=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Somanouchi Kofun cluster'''|杣之内古墳群|}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Somanouchi-cho, Magata-cho, and Otogi-cho neighborhoods of the city of [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The Nishiyama Kofun was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927, and the Nishinokura Kofun was added to the designation in 2018, with the name of the National Historic Site changing from the "Nishiyama Kofun" to the "Somanouchi Kofun cluster".<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210780|title=杣之内古墳群 / 西山古墳 / 西乗鞍古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== Located south of the Furu River, the tumuli are built on hills to the south of [[Isonokami Jingu|Isonokami Shrine]]. The shape of the mounds is characterized by a transition from [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped]] to giant circular tombs. From the location, there is a theory that these the tombs of the chiefs of the [[Mononobe clan]].

===Nishiyama Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishiyama Kofun'''|西山古墳|}} is located on the grounds of Tenri High School ({{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E|display=inline}}). It is believed to have been built in the early Kofun period (4th century), making it the oldest of the Somanouchi Kofun Group. The tumulus has a unique shape, with the lowest tier having a two-conjoined rectangle {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shape, and the central and upper tiers having a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} shaped, which look like a keyhole with one square end and one circular end when viewed from above. This is the only known example of such a design in Japan. The lowest tier consists of two square mounds measuring 90 meters on each side. The middle tier has a circular posterior section 72 meters in diameter, and a rectangular anterior portion with a length of 90 meters. The top of the tumulus is flat, with a diameter of 30 meters and continues with a long and narrow front section for seven meters. The rear of the tumulus is 15 meters high and the front section is ten meters high. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is believed to have been a vertical entry pit-type stone chamber, which has been robbed, most recently in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], when the [[:en:Imperial Japanese Army|Imperial Japanese Army]] set up an anti-aircraft gun emplacement and removed many of the stones. The tumulus is orientated to the west, and was once surrounded by a stepped square moat, which has now been filled-in. The tumulus is located on a gentle slope, making effective use of the natural terrain, so the width of the moat is not constant.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Fragments of a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], jasper arrowheads, tubular beads, and fragments of an iron sword and an iron knife have been excavated from the posterior circular portion. A [[:en:Jasper|Jasper]] wheel stone was discovered in the moat on the south side of the anterior portion. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and fragments of ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found, including cylindrical ''haniwa'', finned cylindrical ''haniwa'', and house-shaped ''haniwa''.
<gallery> Nishiyama Kofun, zenkei-1.jpg|Nishiyama Kofun 080515 Nishiyama Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view of NIshiyama Kofun </gallery>
The tumulus is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tenri Station|Tenri Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sakurai Line|Sakurai Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Nishinorikura Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishinorikura Kofun'''|西乗鞍古墳|}} is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} keyhole-shaped tumulus built by cutting into the foot of a hill stretching from east-to-west on the eastern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]] ({{coord|34|35|9.68|N|135|50|44.56|E|display=inline}}). It is the second largest in the Somanouchi Kofun Group after the Nishiyama Kofun. The tumulus is orientated to the south and is constructed in two tiers. The posterior circular portion has a diameter of 66 meters and height of 15.5 meters; the anterior rectangular port has a width of 88 meters and height of 16 meters. The tumulus was used as an observation point by [[:en:Emperor Hirohito|Emperor Hirohito]] in 1932, when he observed military training maneuvers in the vicinity, and has been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] four times since 1981. Cylindrical and figurative ''haniwa'' clay figures have been found on the outside of the tumulus. There are also projections on the east and west sides of the narrow part of the tumulus. The mound is surrounded by an inner moat and an outer bank (now buried and forming a flat area), and the total length including these is about 165 meters from north-to-south. Furthermore, portions of outer moats are also found on the south and east sides, so it is believed that the tumulus originally had a double moat. The burial chamber is unclear, but it is possible that it was a horizontal-entry stone chamber. The grave goods are also unclear, but [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] pottery have been found with the excavated ''haniwa''. From this pottery, the construction date of the tumulus is estimated to be around the end of the 5th century, or the end of the middle Kofun period.
<gallery> Nishinorikura Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|View of the rear mound from the front part Nishinorikura Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View of the front part from the rear mound 西乗鞍古墳出土 円筒埴輪.JPG|Cylindrical Haniwa excavated from Nishinorikura Kofun </gallery>

From the location, there is a theory that these the tombs of the chiefs of the [[Mononobe clan]].

===Nishiyama Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishiyama Kofun'''|西山古墳|}} is located on the grounds of Tenri High School ({{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E|display=inline}}). It is believed to have been built in the early Kofun period (4th century), making it the oldest of the Somanouchi Kofun Group. The tumulus has a unique shape, with the lowest tier having a two-conjoined rectangle {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shape, and the central and upper tiers having a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} shaped, which look like a keyhole with one square end and one circular end when viewed from above. This is the only known example of such a design in Japan. The lowest tier consists of two square mounds measuring 90 meters on each side. The middle tier has a circular posterior section 72 meters in diameter, and a rectangular anterior portion with a length of 90 meters. The top of the tumulus is flat, with a diameter of 30 meters and continues with a long and narrow front section for seven meters. The rear of the tumulus is 15 meters high and the front section is ten meters high. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is believed to have been a vertical entry pit-type stone chamber, which has been robbed, most recently in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], when the [[:en:Imperial Japanese Army|Imperial Japanese Army]] set up an anti-aircraft gun emplacement and removed many of the stones. The tumulus is orientated to the west, and was once surrounded by a stepped square moat, which has now been filled-in. The tumulus is located on a gentle slope, making effective use of the natural terrain, so the width of the moat is not constant.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Fragments of a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], jasper arrowheads, tubular beads, and fragments of an iron sword and an iron knife have been excavated from the posterior circular portion. A [[:en:Jasper|Jasper]] wheel stone was discovered in the moat on the south side of the anterior portion. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and fragments of ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found, including cylindrical ''haniwa'', finned cylindrical ''haniwa'', and house-shaped ''haniwa''.
<gallery> Nishiyama Kofun, zenkei-1.jpg|Nishiyama Kofun 080515 Nishiyama Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view of NIshiyama Kofun </gallery>
The tumulus is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tenri Station|Tenri Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sakurai Line|Sakurai Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Nishinorikura Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishinorikura Kofun'''|西乗鞍古墳|}} is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} keyhole-shaped tumulus built by cutting into the foot of a hill stretching from east-to-west on the eastern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]] ({{coord|34|35|9.68|N|135|50|44.56|E|display=inline}}). It is the second largest in the Somanouchi Kofun Group after the Nishiyama Kofun. The tumulus is orientated to the south and is constructed in two tiers. The posterior circular portion has a diameter of 66 meters and height of 15.5 meters; the anterior rectangular port has a width of 88 meters and height of 16 meters. The tumulus was used as an observation point by [[:en:Emperor Hirohito|Emperor Hirohito]] in 1932, when he observed military training maneuvers in the vicinity, and has been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] four times since 1981. Cylindrical and figurative ''haniwa'' clay figures have been found on the outside of the tumulus. There are also projections on the east and west sides of the narrow part of the tumulus. The mound is surrounded by an inner moat and an outer bank (now buried and forming a flat area), and the total length including these is about 165 meters from north-to-south. Furthermore, portions of outer moats are also found on the south and east sides, so it is believed that the tumulus originally had a double moat. The burial chamber is unclear, but it is possible that it was a horizontal-entry stone chamber. The grave goods are also unclear, but [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] pottery have been found with the excavated ''haniwa''. From this pottery, the construction date of the tumulus is estimated to be around the end of the 5th century, or the end of the middle Kofun period.
<gallery> Nishinorikura Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|View of the rear mound from the front part Nishinorikura Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View of the front part from the rear mound 西乗鞍古墳出土 円筒埴輪.JPG|Cylindrical Haniwa excavated from Nishinorikura Kofun </gallery>

{{nihongo|'''Somanouchi Kofun cluster'''|杣之内古墳群|}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Somanouchi-cho, Magata-cho, and Otogi-cho neighborhoods of the city of [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The Nishiyama Kofun was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927, and the Nishinokura Kofun was added to the designation in 2018, with the name of the National Historic Site changing from the "Nishiyama Kofun" to the "Somanouchi Kofun cluster".<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210780|title=杣之内古墳群 / 西山古墳 / 西乗鞍古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== Located south of the Furu River, the tumuli are built on hills to the south of [[Isonokami Jingu|Isonokami Shrine]]. The shape of the mounds is characterized by a transition from [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped]] to giant circular tombs. From the location, there is a theory that these the tombs of the chiefs of the [[Mononobe clan]].

===Nishiyama Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishiyama Kofun'''|西山古墳|}} is located on the grounds of Tenri High School ({{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E|display=inline}}). It is believed to have been built in the early Kofun period (4th century), making it the oldest of the Somanouchi Kofun Group. The tumulus has a unique shape, with the lowest tier having a two-conjoined rectangle {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shape, and the central and upper tiers having a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} shaped, which look like a keyhole with one square end and one circular end when viewed from above. This is the only known example of such a design in Japan. The lowest tier consists of two square mounds measuring 90 meters on each side. The middle tier has a circular posterior section 72 meters in diameter, and a rectangular anterior portion with a length of 90 meters. The top of the tumulus is flat, with a diameter of 30 meters and continues with a long and narrow front section for seven meters. The rear of the tumulus is 15 meters high and the front section is ten meters high. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is believed to have been a vertical entry pit-type stone chamber, which has been robbed, most recently in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], when the [[:en:Imperial Japanese Army|Imperial Japanese Army]] set up an anti-aircraft gun emplacement and removed many of the stones. The tumulus is orientated to the west, and was once surrounded by a stepped square moat, which has now been filled-in. The tumulus is located on a gentle slope, making effective use of the natural terrain, so the width of the moat is not constant.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Fragments of a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], jasper arrowheads, tubular beads, and fragments of an iron sword and an iron knife have been excavated from the posterior circular portion. A [[:en:Jasper|Jasper]] wheel stone was discovered in the moat on the south side of the anterior portion. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and fragments of ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found, including cylindrical ''haniwa'', finned cylindrical ''haniwa'', and house-shaped ''haniwa''.
<gallery> Nishiyama Kofun, zenkei-1.jpg|Nishiyama Kofun 080515 Nishiyama Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view of NIshiyama Kofun </gallery>
The tumulus is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tenri Station|Tenri Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sakurai Line|Sakurai Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Nishinorikura Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishinorikura Kofun'''|西乗鞍古墳|}} is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} keyhole-shaped tumulus built by cutting into the foot of a hill stretching from east-to-west on the eastern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]] ({{coord|34|35|9.68|N|135|50|44.56|E|display=inline}}). It is the second largest in the Somanouchi Kofun Group after the Nishiyama Kofun. The tumulus is orientated to the south and is constructed in two tiers. The posterior circular portion has a diameter of 66 meters and height of 15.5 meters; the anterior rectangular port has a width of 88 meters and height of 16 meters. The tumulus was used as an observation point by [[:en:Emperor Hirohito|Emperor Hirohito]] in 1932, when he observed military training maneuvers in the vicinity, and has been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] four times since 1981. Cylindrical and figurative ''haniwa'' clay figures have been found on the outside of the tumulus. There are also projections on the east and west sides of the narrow part of the tumulus. The mound is surrounded by an inner moat and an outer bank (now buried and forming a flat area), and the total length including these is about 165 meters from north-to-south. Furthermore, portions of outer moats are also found on the south and east sides, so it is believed that the tumulus originally had a double moat. The burial chamber is unclear, but it is possible that it was a horizontal-entry stone chamber. The grave goods are also unclear, but [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] pottery have been found with the excavated ''haniwa''. From this pottery, the construction date of the tumulus is estimated to be around the end of the 5th century, or the end of the middle Kofun period.

Kofun|峯塚古墳|}}'''||{{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} || late Kofun period||35 meters || |- |'''{{nihongo|Hoshozuka Kofun|保昌塚古墳|}}'''||{{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} || late Kofun period||25-35 meters ||now partly destroyed |}
===Nishiyama Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishiyama Kofun'''|西山古墳|}} is located on the grounds of Tenri High School ({{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E|display=inline}}). It is believed to have been built in the early Kofun period (4th century), making it the oldest of the Somanouchi Kofun Group. The tumulus has a unique shape, with the lowest tier having a two-conjoined rectangle {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shape, and the central and upper tiers having a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} shaped, which look like a keyhole with one square end and one circular end when viewed from above. This is the only known example of such a design in Japan. The lowest tier consists of two square mounds measuring 90 meters on each side. The middle tier has a circular posterior section 72 meters in diameter, and a rectangular anterior portion with a length of 90 meters. The top of the tumulus is flat, with a diameter of 30 meters and continues with a long and narrow front section for seven meters. The rear of the tumulus is 15 meters high and the front section is ten meters high. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is believed to have been a vertical entry pit-type stone chamber, which has been robbed, most recently in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], when the [[:en:Imperial Japanese Army|Imperial Japanese Army]] set up an anti-aircraft gun emplacement and removed many of the stones. The tumulus is orientated to the west, and was once surrounded by a stepped square moat, which has now been filled-in. The tumulus is located on a gentle slope, making effective use of the natural terrain, so the width of the moat is not constant.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Fragments of a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], jasper arrowheads, tubular beads, and fragments of an iron sword and an iron knife have been excavated from the posterior circular portion. A [[:en:Jasper|Jasper]] wheel stone was discovered in the moat on the south side of the anterior portion. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and fragments of ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found, including cylindrical ''haniwa'', finned cylindrical ''haniwa'', and house-shaped ''haniwa''.
<gallery> Nishiyama Kofun, zenkei-1.jpg|Nishiyama Kofun 080515 Nishiyama Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view of NIshiyama Kofun </gallery>
The tumulus is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tenri Station|Tenri Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sakurai Line|Sakurai Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Nishinorikura Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishinorikura Kofun'''|西乗鞍古墳|}} is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} keyhole-shaped tumulus built by cutting into the foot of a hill stretching from east-to-west on the eastern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]] ({{coord|34|35|9.68|N|135|50|44.56|E|display=inline}}). It is the second largest in the Somanouchi Kofun Group after the Nishiyama Kofun. The tumulus is orientated to the south and is constructed in two tiers. The posterior circular portion has a diameter of 66 meters and height of 15.5 meters; the anterior rectangular port has a width of 88 meters and height of 16 meters. The tumulus was used as an observation point by [[:en:Emperor Hirohito|Emperor Hirohito]] in 1932, when he observed military training maneuvers in the vicinity, and has been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] four times since 1981. Cylindrical and figurative ''haniwa'' clay figures have been found on the outside of the tumulus. There are also projections on the east and west sides of the narrow part of the tumulus. The mound is surrounded by an inner moat and an outer bank (now buried and forming a flat area), and the total length including these is about 165 meters from north-to-south. Furthermore, portions of outer moats are also found on the south and east sides, so it is believed that the tumulus originally had a double moat. The burial chamber is unclear, but it is possible that it was a horizontal-entry stone chamber. The grave goods are also unclear, but [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] pottery have been found with the excavated ''haniwa''. From this pottery, the construction date of the tumulus is estimated to be around the end of the 5th century, or the end of the middle Kofun period.
<gallery> Nishinorikura Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|View of the rear mound from the front part Nishinorikura Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View of the front part from the rear mound 西乗鞍古墳出土 円筒埴輪.JPG|Cylindrical Haniwa excavated from Nishinorikura Kofun </gallery>

{{Coord|34.59275|N|135.84366666666668|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location =[[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Tenri|WD=Q502196}}| region = [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th- 7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Somanouchi Kofun cluster'''|杣之内古墳群|}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Somanouchi-cho, Magata-cho, and Otogi-cho neighborhoods of the city of [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The Nishiyama Kofun was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927, and the Nishinokura Kofun was added to the designation in 2018, with the name of the National Historic Site changing from the "Nishiyama Kofun" to the "Somanouchi Kofun cluster".<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210780|title=杣之内古墳群 / 西山古墳 / 西乗鞍古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== Located south of the Furu River, the tumuli are built on hills to the south of [[Isonokami Jingu|Isonokami Shrine]]. The shape of the mounds is characterized by a transition from [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped]] to giant circular tombs. From the location, there is a theory that these the tombs of the chiefs of the [[Mononobe clan]].

===Nishiyama Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishiyama Kofun'''|西山古墳|}} is located on the grounds of Tenri High School ({{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E|display=inline}}). It is believed to have been built in the early Kofun period (4th century), making it the oldest of the Somanouchi Kofun Group. The tumulus has a unique shape, with the lowest tier having a two-conjoined rectangle {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shape, and the central and upper tiers having a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} shaped, which look like a keyhole with one square end and one circular end when viewed from above. This is the only known example of such a design in Japan. The lowest tier consists of two square mounds measuring 90 meters on each side. The middle tier has a circular posterior section 72 meters in diameter, and a rectangular anterior portion with a length of 90 meters. The top of the tumulus is flat, with a diameter of 30 meters and continues with a long and narrow front section for seven meters. The rear of the tumulus is 15 meters high and the front section is ten meters high. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is believed to have been a vertical entry pit-type stone chamber, which has been robbed, most recently in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], when the [[:en:Imperial Japanese Army|Imperial Japanese Army]] set up an anti-aircraft gun emplacement and removed many of the stones. The tumulus is located on a gentle slope, making effective use of the natural terrain, so the width of the moat is not constant.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>

''[[Fukiishi]]'' and fragments of ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found, including cylindrical ''haniwa'', finned cylindrical ''haniwa'', and house-shaped ''haniwa''.
<gallery> Nishiyama Kofun, zenkei-1.jpg|Nishiyama Kofun 080515 Nishiyama Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view of NIshiyama Kofun </gallery>
The tumulus is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tenri Station|Tenri Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sakurai Line|Sakurai Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Nishinorikura Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishinorikura Kofun'''|西乗鞍古墳|}} is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} keyhole-shaped tumulus built by cutting into the foot of a hill stretching from east-to-west on the eastern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]] ({{coord|34|35|9.68|N|135|50|44.56|E|display=inline}}). {{nihongo|'''Somanouchi Kofun cluster'''|杣之内古墳群|}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Somanouchi-cho, Magata-cho, and Otogi-cho neighborhoods of the city of [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The Nishiyama Kofun was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927, and the Nishinokura Kofun was added to the designation in 2018, with the name of the National Historic Site changing from the "Nishiyama Kofun" to the "Somanouchi Kofun cluster".<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210780|title=杣之内古墳群 / 西山古墳 / 西乗鞍古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== Located south of the Furu River, the tumuli are built on hills to the south of [[Isonokami Jingu|Isonokami Shrine]]. The shape of the mounds is characterized by a transition from [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped]] to giant circular tombs. From the location, there is a theory that these the tombs of the chiefs of the [[Mononobe clan]].

Kofun}} || late Kofun period||25-35 meters ||now partly destroyed |}
===Nishiyama Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishiyama Kofun'''|西山古墳|}} is located on the grounds of Tenri High School ({{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E|display=inline}}). It is believed to have been built in the early Kofun period (4th century), making it the oldest of the Somanouchi Kofun Group. The tumulus has a unique shape, with the lowest tier having a two-conjoined rectangle {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shape, and the central and upper tiers having a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} shaped, which look like a keyhole with one square end and one circular end when viewed from above. This is the only known example of such a design in Japan. The lowest tier consists of two square mounds measuring 90 meters on each side. The middle tier has a circular posterior section 72 meters in diameter, and a rectangular anterior portion with a length of 90 meters. The top of the tumulus is flat, with a diameter of 30 meters and continues with a long and narrow front section for seven meters. The rear of the tumulus is 15 meters high and the front section is ten meters high. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is believed to have been a vertical entry pit-type stone chamber, which has been robbed, most recently in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], when the [[:en:Imperial Japanese Army|Imperial Japanese Army]] set up an anti-aircraft gun emplacement and removed many of the stones. The tumulus is orientated to the west, and was once surrounded by a stepped square moat, which has now been filled-in. The tumulus is located on a gentle slope, making effective use of the natural terrain, so the width of the moat is not constant.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Fragments of a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], jasper arrowheads, tubular beads, and fragments of an iron sword and an iron knife have been excavated from the posterior circular portion. A [[:en:Jasper|Jasper]] wheel stone was discovered in the moat on the south side of the anterior portion. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and fragments of ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found, including cylindrical ''haniwa'', finned cylindrical ''haniwa'', and house-shaped ''haniwa''.
<gallery> Nishiyama Kofun, zenkei-1.jpg|Nishiyama Kofun 080515 Nishiyama Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view of NIshiyama Kofun </gallery>
The tumulus is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tenri Station|Tenri Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sakurai Line|Sakurai Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Nishinorikura Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishinorikura Kofun'''|西乗鞍古墳|}} is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} keyhole-shaped tumulus built by cutting into the foot of a hill stretching from east-to-west on the eastern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]] ({{coord|34|35|9.68|N|135|50|44.56|E|display=inline}}). It is the second largest in the Somanouchi Kofun Group after the Nishiyama Kofun. The tumulus is orientated to the south and is constructed in two tiers. The posterior circular portion has a diameter of 66 meters and height of 15.5 meters; the anterior rectangular port has a width of 88 meters and height of 16 meters. The tumulus was used as an observation point by [[:en:Emperor Hirohito|Emperor Hirohito]] in 1932, when he observed military training maneuvers in the vicinity, and has been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] four times since 1981. Cylindrical and figurative ''haniwa'' clay figures have been found on the outside of the tumulus. There are also projections on the east and west sides of the narrow part of the tumulus. The mound is surrounded by an inner moat and an outer bank (now buried and forming a flat area), and the total length including these is about 165 meters from north-to-south. Furthermore, portions of outer moats are also found on the south and east sides, so it is believed that the tumulus originally had a double moat. The burial chamber is unclear, but it is possible that it was a horizontal-entry stone chamber. The grave goods are also unclear, but [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] pottery have been found with the excavated ''haniwa''. From this pottery, the construction date of the tumulus is estimated to be around the end of the 5th century, or the end of the middle Kofun period.
<gallery> Nishinorikura Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|View of the rear mound from the front part Nishinorikura Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View of the front part from the rear mound 西乗鞍古墳出土 円筒埴輪.JPG|Cylindrical Haniwa excavated from Nishinorikura Kofun </gallery>

{{Coord|34.59275|N|135.84366666666668|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location =[[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Tenri|WD=Q502196}}| region = [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th- 7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Somanouchi Kofun cluster'''|杣之内古墳群|}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Somanouchi-cho, Magata-cho, and Otogi-cho neighborhoods of the city of [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The Nishiyama Kofun was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927, and the Nishinokura Kofun was added to the designation in 2018, with the name of the National Historic Site changing from the "Nishiyama Kofun" to the "Somanouchi Kofun cluster".<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210780|title=杣之内古墳群 / 西山古墳 / 西乗鞍古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== Located south of the Furu River, the tumuli are built on hills to the south of [[Isonokami Jingu|Isonokami Shrine]]. The shape of the mounds is characterized by a transition from [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped]] to giant circular tombs. From the location, there is a theory that these the tombs of the chiefs of the [[Mononobe clan]].

===Nishiyama Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishiyama Kofun'''|西山古墳|}} is located on the grounds of Tenri High School ({{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E|display=inline}}). It is believed to have been built in the early Kofun period (4th century), making it the oldest of the Somanouchi Kofun Group. The tumulus has a unique shape, with the lowest tier having a two-conjoined rectangle {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shape, and the central and upper tiers having a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} shaped, which look like a keyhole with one square end and one circular end when viewed from above. <gallery> Nishiyama Kofun, zenkei-1.jpg|Nishiyama Kofun 080515 Nishiyama Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view of NIshiyama Kofun </gallery>
The tumulus is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tenri Station|Tenri Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sakurai Line|Sakurai Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Nishinorikura Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishinorikura Kofun'''|西乗鞍古墳|}} is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} keyhole-shaped tumulus built by cutting into the foot of a hill stretching from east-to-west on the eastern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]] ({{coord|34|35|9.68|N|135|50|44.56|E|display=inline}}). {{nihongo|'''Somanouchi Kofun cluster'''|杣之内古墳群|}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Somanouchi-cho, Magata-cho, and Otogi-cho neighborhoods of the city of [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The Nishiyama Kofun was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927, and the Nishinokura Kofun was added to the designation in 2018, with the name of the National Historic Site changing from the "Nishiyama Kofun" to the "Somanouchi Kofun cluster".<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210780|title=杣之内古墳群 / 西山古墳 / 西乗鞍古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== Located south of the Furu River, the tumuli are built on hills to the south of [[Isonokami Jingu|Isonokami Shrine]]. The shape of the mounds is characterized by a transition from [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped]] to giant circular tombs. From the location, there is a theory that these the tombs of the chiefs of the [[Mononobe clan]].

Kofun|峯塚古墳|}}'''||{{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} || late Kofun period||35 meters || |- |'''{{nihongo|Hoshozuka Kofun|保昌塚古墳|}}'''||{{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} || late Kofun period||25-35 meters ||now partly destroyed |}
===Nishiyama Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishiyama Kofun'''|西山古墳|}} is located on the grounds of Tenri High School ({{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E|display=inline}}). It is believed to have been built in the early Kofun period (4th century), making it the oldest of the Somanouchi Kofun Group. The tumulus has a unique shape, with the lowest tier having a two-conjoined rectangle {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shape, and the central and upper tiers having a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} shaped, which look like a keyhole with one square end and one circular end when viewed from above. This is the only known example of such a design in Japan. The lowest tier consists of two square mounds measuring 90 meters on each side. The middle tier has a circular posterior section 72 meters in diameter, and a rectangular anterior portion with a length of 90 meters. The top of the tumulus is flat, with a diameter of 30 meters and continues with a long and narrow front section for seven meters. The rear of the tumulus is 15 meters high and the front section is ten meters high. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is believed to have been a vertical entry pit-type stone chamber, which has been robbed, most recently in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], when the [[:en:Imperial Japanese Army|Imperial Japanese Army]] set up an anti-aircraft gun emplacement and removed many of the stones. The tumulus is orientated to the west, and was once surrounded by a stepped square moat, which has now been filled-in. The tumulus is located on a gentle slope, making effective use of the natural terrain, so the width of the moat is not constant.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Fragments of a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], jasper arrowheads, tubular beads, and fragments of an iron sword and an iron knife have been excavated from the posterior circular portion. A [[:en:Jasper|Jasper]] wheel stone was discovered in the moat on the south side of the anterior portion. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and fragments of ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found, including cylindrical ''haniwa'', finned cylindrical ''haniwa'', and house-shaped ''haniwa''.
<gallery> Nishiyama Kofun, zenkei-1.jpg|Nishiyama Kofun 080515 Nishiyama Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view of NIshiyama Kofun </gallery>
The tumulus is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tenri Station|Tenri Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sakurai Line|Sakurai Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Nishinorikura Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishinorikura Kofun'''|西乗鞍古墳|}} is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} keyhole-shaped tumulus built by cutting into the foot of a hill stretching from east-to-west on the eastern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]] ({{coord|34|35|9.68|N|135|50|44.56|E|display=inline}}). It is the second largest in the Somanouchi Kofun Group after the Nishiyama Kofun. The tumulus is orientated to the south and is constructed in two tiers. The posterior circular portion has a diameter of 66 meters and height of 15.5 meters; the anterior rectangular port has a width of 88 meters and height of 16 meters. The tumulus was used as an observation point by [[:en:Emperor Hirohito|Emperor Hirohito]] in 1932, when he observed military training maneuvers in the vicinity, and has been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] four times since 1981. Cylindrical and figurative ''haniwa'' clay figures have been found on the outside of the tumulus. There are also projections on the east and west sides of the narrow part of the tumulus. The mound is surrounded by an inner moat and an outer bank (now buried and forming a flat area), and the total length including these is about 165 meters from north-to-south. Furthermore, portions of outer moats are also found on the south and east sides, so it is believed that the tumulus originally had a double moat. The burial chamber is unclear, but it is possible that it was a horizontal-entry stone chamber. The grave goods are also unclear, but [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] pottery have been found with the excavated ''haniwa''. From this pottery, the construction date of the tumulus is estimated to be around the end of the 5th century, or the end of the middle Kofun period.
<gallery> Nishinorikura Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|View of the rear mound from the front part Nishinorikura Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View of the front part from the rear mound 西乗鞍古墳出土 円筒埴輪.JPG|Cylindrical Haniwa excavated from Nishinorikura Kofun </gallery>
==See also== *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Nara)]]

===Nishiyama Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishiyama Kofun'''|西山古墳|}} is located on the grounds of Tenri High School ({{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E|display=inline}}). It is believed to have been built in the early Kofun period (4th century), making it the oldest of the Somanouchi Kofun Group. The tumulus has a unique shape, with the lowest tier having a two-conjoined rectangle {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shape, and the central and upper tiers having a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} shaped, which look like a keyhole with one square end and one circular end when viewed from above. This is the only known example of such a design in Japan. The lowest tier consists of two square mounds measuring 90 meters on each side. The middle tier has a circular posterior section 72 meters in diameter, and a rectangular anterior portion with a length of 90 meters. The top of the tumulus is flat, with a diameter of 30 meters and continues with a long and narrow front section for seven meters. The rear of the tumulus is 15 meters high and the front section is ten meters high. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is believed to have been a vertical entry pit-type stone chamber, which has been robbed, most recently in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], when the [[:en:Imperial Japanese Army|Imperial Japanese Army]] set up an anti-aircraft gun emplacement and removed many of the stones. The tumulus is located on a gentle slope, making effective use of the natural terrain, so the width of the moat is not constant.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Fragments of a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], jasper arrowheads, tubular beads, and fragments of an iron sword and an iron knife have been excavated from the posterior circular portion. A [[:en:Jasper|Jasper]] wheel stone was discovered in the moat on the south side of the anterior portion. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and fragments of ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found, including cylindrical ''haniwa'', finned cylindrical ''haniwa'', and house-shaped ''haniwa''.
<gallery> Nishiyama Kofun, zenkei-1.jpg|Nishiyama Kofun 080515 Nishiyama Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view of NIshiyama Kofun </gallery>
The tumulus is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tenri Station|Tenri Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sakurai Line|Sakurai Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Nishinorikura Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishinorikura Kofun'''|西乗鞍古墳|}} is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} keyhole-shaped tumulus built by cutting into the foot of a hill stretching from east-to-west on the eastern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]] ({{coord|34|35|9.68|N|135|50|44.56|E|display=inline}}). It is the second largest in the Somanouchi Kofun Group after the Nishiyama Kofun. The tumulus is orientated to the south and is constructed in two tiers. The posterior circular portion has a diameter of 66 meters and height of 15.5 meters; the anterior rectangular port has a width of 88 meters and height of 16 meters. The tumulus was used as an observation point by [[:en:Emperor Hirohito|Emperor Hirohito]] in 1932, when he observed military training maneuvers in the vicinity, and has been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] four times since 1981. Cylindrical and figurative ''haniwa'' clay figures have been found on the outside of the tumulus. There are also projections on the east and west sides of the narrow part of the tumulus. The mound is surrounded by an inner moat and an outer bank (now buried and forming a flat area), and the total length including these is about 165 meters from north-to-south. Furthermore, portions of outer moats are also found on the south and east sides, so it is believed that the tumulus originally had a double moat. The burial chamber is unclear, but it is possible that it was a horizontal-entry stone chamber. The grave goods are also unclear, but [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] pottery have been found with the excavated ''haniwa''. From this pottery, the construction date of the tumulus is estimated to be around the end of the 5th century, or the end of the middle Kofun period.
<gallery> Nishinorikura Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|View of the rear mound from the front part Nishinorikura Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View of the front part from the rear mound 西乗鞍古墳出土 円筒埴輪.JPG|Cylindrical Haniwa excavated from Nishinorikura Kofun </gallery>

{{nihongo|'''Somanouchi Kofun cluster'''|杣之内古墳群|}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Somanouchi-cho, Magata-cho, and Otogi-cho neighborhoods of the city of [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The Nishiyama Kofun was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927, and the Nishinokura Kofun was added to the designation in 2018, with the name of the National Historic Site changing from the "Nishiyama Kofun" to the "Somanouchi Kofun cluster".<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210780|title=杣之内古墳群 / 西山古墳 / 西乗鞍古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== Located south of the Furu River, the tumuli are built on hills to the south of [[Isonokami Jingu|Isonokami Shrine]]. The shape of the mounds is characterized by a transition from [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped]] to giant circular tombs. From the location, there is a theory that these the tombs of the chiefs of the [[Mononobe clan]].

===Nishiyama Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishiyama Kofun'''|西山古墳|}} is located on the grounds of Tenri High School ({{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E|display=inline}}). It is believed to have been built in the early Kofun period (4th century), making it the oldest of the Somanouchi Kofun Group. The tumulus has a unique shape, with the lowest tier having a two-conjoined rectangle {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shape, and the central and upper tiers having a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} shaped, which look like a keyhole with one square end and one circular end when viewed from above. This is the only known example of such a design in Japan. The lowest tier consists of two square mounds measuring 90 meters on each side. The middle tier has a circular posterior section 72 meters in diameter, and a rectangular anterior portion with a length of 90 meters. The top of the tumulus is flat, with a diameter of 30 meters and continues with a long and narrow front section for seven meters. The rear of the tumulus is 15 meters high and the front section is ten meters high. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is believed to have been a vertical entry pit-type stone chamber, which has been robbed, most recently in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], when the [[:en:Imperial Japanese Army|Imperial Japanese Army]] set up an anti-aircraft gun emplacement and removed many of the stones. The tumulus is orientated to the west, and was once surrounded by a stepped square moat, which has now been filled-in. The tumulus is located on a gentle slope, making effective use of the natural terrain, so the width of the moat is not constant.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Fragments of a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], jasper arrowheads, tubular beads, and fragments of an iron sword and an iron knife have been excavated from the posterior circular portion. A [[:en:Jasper|Jasper]] wheel stone was discovered in the moat on the south side of the anterior portion. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and fragments of ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found, including cylindrical ''haniwa'', finned cylindrical ''haniwa'', and house-shaped ''haniwa''.
<gallery> Nishiyama Kofun, zenkei-1.jpg|Nishiyama Kofun 080515 Nishiyama Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view of NIshiyama Kofun </gallery>
The tumulus is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tenri Station|Tenri Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sakurai Line|Sakurai Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Nishinorikura Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishinorikura Kofun'''|西乗鞍古墳|}} is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} keyhole-shaped tumulus built by cutting into the foot of a hill stretching from east-to-west on the eastern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]] ({{coord|34|35|9.68|N|135|50|44.56|E|display=inline}}). It is the second largest in the Somanouchi Kofun Group after the Nishiyama Kofun. The tumulus is orientated to the south and is constructed in two tiers. The posterior circular portion has a diameter of 66 meters and height of 15.5 meters; the anterior rectangular port has a width of 88 meters and height of 16 meters. The tumulus was used as an observation point by [[:en:Emperor Hirohito|Emperor Hirohito]] in 1932, when he observed military training maneuvers in the vicinity, and has been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] four times since 1981. Cylindrical and figurative ''haniwa'' clay figures have been found on the outside of the tumulus. There are also projections on the east and west sides of the narrow part of the tumulus. The mound is surrounded by an inner moat and an outer bank (now buried and forming a flat area), and the total length including these is about 165 meters from north-to-south. Furthermore, portions of outer moats are also found on the south and east sides, so it is believed that the tumulus originally had a double moat. The burial chamber is unclear, but it is possible that it was a horizontal-entry stone chamber. The grave goods are also unclear, but [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] pottery have been found with the excavated ''haniwa''. From this pottery, the construction date of the tumulus is estimated to be around the end of the 5th century, or the end of the middle Kofun period.

Kofun|峯塚古墳|}}'''||{{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} || late Kofun period||35 meters || |- |'''{{nihongo|Hoshozuka Kofun|保昌塚古墳|}}'''||{{nihongo|''enpun''|円墳|1=Round Kofun|1=Round Kofun}} || late Kofun period||25-35 meters ||now partly destroyed |}
===Nishiyama Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishiyama Kofun'''|西山古墳|}} is located on the grounds of Tenri High School ({{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E|display=inline}}). It is believed to have been built in the early Kofun period (4th century), making it the oldest of the Somanouchi Kofun Group. The tumulus has a unique shape, with the lowest tier having a two-conjoined rectangle {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōhō-fun''|前方後方墳|lt=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|1=Zenpō-kōhō-fun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=zenpō-kōhō-fun}} shape, and the central and upper tiers having a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} shaped, which look like a keyhole with one square end and one circular end when viewed from above. This is the only known example of such a design in Japan. The lowest tier consists of two square mounds measuring 90 meters on each side. The middle tier has a circular posterior section 72 meters in diameter, and a rectangular anterior portion with a length of 90 meters. The top of the tumulus is flat, with a diameter of 30 meters and continues with a long and narrow front section for seven meters. The rear of the tumulus is 15 meters high and the front section is ten meters high. The [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]] is believed to have been a vertical entry pit-type stone chamber, which has been robbed, most recently in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], when the [[:en:Imperial Japanese Army|Imperial Japanese Army]] set up an anti-aircraft gun emplacement and removed many of the stones. The tumulus is orientated to the west, and was once surrounded by a stepped square moat, which has now been filled-in. The tumulus is located on a gentle slope, making effective use of the natural terrain, so the width of the moat is not constant.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=978-4311750403}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
Fragments of a [[:en:Bronze mirror|Bronze mirror]], jasper arrowheads, tubular beads, and fragments of an iron sword and an iron knife have been excavated from the posterior circular portion. A [[:en:Jasper|Jasper]] wheel stone was discovered in the moat on the south side of the anterior portion. ''[[Fukiishi]]'' and fragments of ''[[:en:Haniwa|Haniwa]]'' have been found, including cylindrical ''haniwa'', finned cylindrical ''haniwa'', and house-shaped ''haniwa''.
<gallery> Nishiyama Kofun, zenkei-1.jpg|Nishiyama Kofun 080515 Nishiyama Kofun aerial.jpg|Aerial view of NIshiyama Kofun </gallery>
The tumulus is about a 20-minute walk from [[:en:Tenri Station|Tenri Station]] on the [[:en:West Japan Railway Company|West Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Sakurai Line|Sakurai Line]].<ref name="Shiseki"/>
===Nishinorikura Kofun=== The {{nihongo|'''Nishinorikura Kofun'''|西乗鞍古墳|}} is a {{nihongo|''zenpō-kōen-fun''|前方後円墳|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpokoenfun|comment=doing this because I need to differentiate it in piped links too with my new interwiki tool as not the ambiguous phrase keyhole shaped kofun|1=Zenpokoenfun|1=[[Zenpokoenfun]]|2=前方後円墳|3=|4=}} keyhole-shaped tumulus built by cutting into the foot of a hill stretching from east-to-west on the eastern edge of the [[:en:Nara Basin|Nara Basin]] ({{coord|34|35|9.68|N|135|50|44.56|E|display=inline}}). It is the second largest in the Somanouchi Kofun Group after the Nishiyama Kofun. The tumulus is orientated to the south and is constructed in two tiers. The posterior circular portion has a diameter of 66 meters and height of 15.5 meters; the anterior rectangular port has a width of 88 meters and height of 16 meters. The tumulus was used as an observation point by [[:en:Emperor Hirohito|Emperor Hirohito]] in 1932, when he observed military training maneuvers in the vicinity, and has been [[archaeological excavation|excavated]] four times since 1981. Cylindrical and figurative ''haniwa'' clay figures have been found on the outside of the tumulus. There are also projections on the east and west sides of the narrow part of the tumulus. The mound is surrounded by an inner moat and an outer bank (now buried and forming a flat area), and the total length including these is about 165 meters from north-to-south. Furthermore, portions of outer moats are also found on the south and east sides, so it is believed that the tumulus originally had a double moat. The burial chamber is unclear, but it is possible that it was a horizontal-entry stone chamber. The grave goods are also unclear, but [[:en:Sue pottery|Sue pottery]] and [[:en:Haji pottery|Haji pottery]] pottery have been found with the excavated ''haniwa''. From this pottery, the construction date of the tumulus is estimated to be around the end of the 5th century, or the end of the middle Kofun period.
<gallery> Nishinorikura Kofun, kouenbu.jpg|View of the rear mound from the front part Nishinorikura Kofun, zenpoubu.jpg|View of the front part from the rear mound 西乗鞍古墳出土 円筒埴輪.JPG|Cylindrical Haniwa excavated from Nishinorikura Kofun </gallery>

{{Coord|34.59275|N|135.84366666666668|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}| map_dot_label = | location =[[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]; {{ill|Tenri|WD=Q502196}}| region = [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length = | width = | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = c.4th- 7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site =; {{ill|Historic Site of Japan|WD=Q30834580}}| website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=14|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|35|33|N|135|50|36|E}}}}}}
{{nihongo|'''Somanouchi Kofun cluster'''|杣之内古墳群|}} is a cluster of [[Kofun period]] [[Kofun|Kofun]]s, located in the Somanouchi-cho, Magata-cho, and Otogi-cho neighborhoods of the city of [[:en:Tenri, Nara|Tenri, Nara]] in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] of [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. The Nishiyama Kofun was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 1927, and the Nishinokura Kofun was added to the designation in 2018, with the name of the National Historic Site changing from the "Nishiyama Kofun" to the "Somanouchi Kofun cluster".<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/210780|title=杣之内古墳群 / 西山古墳 / 西乗鞍古墳|language=ja |publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs |accessdate=August 20, 2024}}</ref>
==Overview== Located south of the Furu River, the tumuli are built on hills to the south of [[Isonokami Jingu|Isonokami Shrine]]. The shape of the mounds is characterized by a transition from [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|keyhole-shaped]] to giant circular tombs. From the location, there is a theory that these the tombs of the chiefs of the [[Mononobe clan]].
